clean_story:
Floating graveyards. Silent breakdown.

The term plastic’s ocean afterlife describes what happens when plastic escapes recycling—drift, fracture, and vanish into ecosystems, never truly disappearing.

A water bottle tossed in New York can ride currents to the Arctic in two years, not as a bottle but as confetti. Sunlight and waves act like a cheese grater, shaving it into microplastics—fragments smaller than a sesame seed. These don’t dissolve; they just become invisible to the eye, like sugar in tea. Zooplankton mistake them for algae, eating up to 12,000 pieces daily. Fish ingest those plastified plankton, then seagulls eat the fish, and so on—each step concentrating toxins like DDT (banned in 1972 but still clinging to plastic). By 2025, researchers estimate the ocean will contain one ton of plastic for every three tons of fish.

Some plastics sink, forming underwater snowdrifts in the Mariana Trench. Others wash ashore as "mermaid tears"—nurdles, the raw pellet form, spilled by cargo ships. On Henderson Island, an uninhabited Pacific atoll, 18 tons of plastic accumulate yearly, delivered by currents like a conveyor belt. No humans live there. The plastic does.

Three related subjects are ghost nets’ phantom fishing, bacteria eating polyethylene, and how tides sort trash.
